commits.message:
mit-styling-aj1.9: the close — a nine-blade iris stops down out of the descent, gathering the journey's light to one focal point where the last line resolves

The lens motif that runs the whole piece (focus rack, snow bokeh, zoom loupe,
depth dive) comes to rest: a mechanical aperture stops down. Nine faceted blades
sweep the field inward, tiling the annulus between the opening N-gon and the
housing so the aperture is simply the untiled hole; the focal core concentrates
and the struck rim glows as the opening seats. The closing line resolves sharp
at the focus — replacing "Scroll in. Picture out." (bmf: doesn't land) with
"One thread — all the way down.", which names the node's own metaphor and
bookends the descent's recurring refrain.

Distinct from every other section's painter: a hard bladed iris, not snow's soft
bokeh or mask's straight cut. Pure geometry recomputed each frame from scroll p
(opening radius + mechanical turn) — same ops every frame, variability in the
values [LAW:dataflow-not-control-flow] — so it is reduced-motion-safe by
construction; only the spin is gated (mo). No getTotalLength, no dash-draw:
sidesteps the draw-on bug class entirely. Treatment confined to the converge
painter + .iz CSS block; the shared paint loop is untouched.

Verified in Chrome across the full scroll range (open / mid-close / seated):
zero console messages, geometry scrubs cleanly, the line resolves at the focus.
mit-styling-aj1.8.4: the data essay — the scatter constructs itself into an annotated Pudding chart, two colour-coded series resolving onto a trend with an editorial callout (#42)
